old_title: Iran war latest: Trump threatens 'major punishment' on Iran after Houthi attack
new_title: Iran-US war: Trump is in 'revenge mode' after losing patience, allies say
old_text:
Donald Trump has threatened a "major military punishment" against Iran after Yemen's Iran-backed Houthi rebels bombed two Saudi oil tankers off the coast of Saudi Arabia.
The attacks threaten to further disrupt oil supply in a sign that the war is spreading across the region, as the Houthis warn they will cut off the Bab al-Mandab strait, one of the critical waterways through which oil flows.
"The U.S. will hold Iran responsible, in that the Houthis are a Surrogate and/or Proxy of Iran, and major military punishment will be inflicted upon Iran," the US president wrote on Truth Social.
Trump also threatened punishment fore the Houthis, who he said he is "very disappointed with in that they have, until now, acted very professionally and smart".
It comes as US secretary of state Marco Rubio has claimed Iran is "begging for a deal" amid escalating tensions across the Middle East, while warning that Tehran will face intensifying US strikes "every night until they come to their senses".
The US military said it had carried out a 12th straight night of strikes against Iran in the early hours of Thursday, including the first use of B-1 long-range bombers in weeks.
Iran's Revolutionary Guard defends its strikes on Gulf states
Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) defended its attacks on US military bases in Kuwait and Jordan, arguing that the strikes were aimed at American forces rather than the Gulf states themselves.
Responding to accusations that Iran had violated the sovereignty of countries where US bases are located, the IRGC said such claims were "completely incorrect", according to Mehr state news agency.
In a message to Kuwait, it said: "It is America that has violated your territorial integrity and sovereignty, not us."
The IRGC argued that US military bases in Gulf countries were effectively under American control and claimed local officials could not access them without US permission.
It said: "We are attacking lands occupied by the US army."
The group added that the bases were being used to carry out "crimes" against Iran and said: "It is our legal, religious and logical right to target the invader of our country."
In a separate statement directed at Jordan, the IRGC said: "In our opinion, the independence, territorial integrity and sovereignty of your government are completely respected."
Trump threatens 'major military punishment' against Iran after Houthi attack
Donald Trump has threatened a "major military punishment" against Iran, holding it responsible for attacks by Yemen's Iran-backed Houthi rebels bombed two Saudi oil tankers off the coast of Saudi Arabia.
The attacks threaten to further disrupt oil supply in a sign that the war is spreading across the region, as the Houthis warn they will cut off the Bab al-Mandab strait, one of the critical waterways through which oil flows.
"The U.S. will hold Iran responsible, in that the Houthis are a Surrogate and/or Proxy of Iran, and major military punishment will be inflicted upon Iran, and, of course, the Houthis, themselves, who I am very disappointed with in that they have, until now, acted very professionally and smart," the US president wrote on Truth Social.
Iran showed its 'true colours' by attacking neighbours during war with US, American envoy to UK says
The US ambassador to the UK, Warren Stephens, has said that Iran has shown its "true colours" by attacking several Arab countries during its war with the US.
Stephens told Sky News that the duration of renewed US attacks on Iran would "depend on Iran", particularly whether Tehran continues disrupting shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
He said that "they've shown their hand" by attacking regional neighbours and "hopefully that's going to isolate this... regime".
Earlier, responding to accusations that Iran had violated the sovereignty of countries where US bases are located, Iran's Revolutionary Guard said such claims were "completely incorrect", according to Mehr state news agency.
In a message to Kuwait, it said: "It is America that has violated your territorial integrity and sovereignty, not us."
Pakistan PM condemns Houthi attacks on Saudi oil tankers
Pakistan's prime minister Shehbaz Sharif has condemned the Houthi attacks on Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea, calling them unacceptable and a violation of international law.
Sharif wrote on X that he conveyed this directly to Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman and reaffirmed that Pakistan stands firmly with Saudi Arabia.
"I condemned, in the strongest possible terms, the Houthi militia's attacks against Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea. Such actions are unacceptable, violate international law, threaten freedom of navigation, and undermine regional peace and security.
"I reaffirmed Pakistan's complete solidarity with the brotherly Kingdom of Saudi Arabia and conveyed that I, together with Field Marshal Syed Asim Munir and the entire Pakistani nation, stand firmly and resolutely with the Saudi leadership and the brotherly people of the Kingdom at this critical time."
Iran could be the richest country in the Middle East, Rubio says
US secretary of state Marco Rubio has said that Iran's "lunatic" leaders, including the clerics, are holding the country back and that it could be "the richest country in the Middle East".
"Some of them are lunatics, some of them are evil, some of them are evil lunatics," he told reporters. He then added: "You can be both."
He said that Iran "is a country run by radical clerics," who are "oblivious" to economic issues.
"They are destroying their country," he said, adding: "But instead they take their money, and they use it and they give it to Hezbollah, they give it to Hamas, they give it to the Houthis. They give it to Shia militias. They give it to sponsored terrorism all over the world."
Rubio also repeated the US position that Iran can't have a nuclear weapon. "They cannot have nuclear weapons; I mean, imagine what they would do to the world," he said.
US uses B-1 long-range bomber for first time since fighting against Iran resumed
The US has escalated its military campaign against Iran by using a B-1 long-range bomber for the first time since fighting resumed 12 days ago, according to US officials.
The bomber was used to strike IRGC targets in Iran, Axios reported. B-1 aircraft can carry large numbers of heavy bombs and cruise missiles.
US Centcom said its strikes targeted Iranian military operations centres, maritime capabilities, aircraft hangars, drone storage facilities and logistics infrastructure to weaken Tehran's ability to threaten commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.
However, it remains unclear what specific targets the B-1 hit.
Rubio claims Iran 'begging for a deal' amid escalating US strikes
The US secretary of state has claimed that Iran is "begging for a deal" with the US and that the "price on Iran will get higher every night until they come to their senses".
Speaking at the ASEAN conference in Manila, Marco Rubio said: "Iran is begging for a deal. It looks like Iran is not ready to make a deal, they will pay the price."
He added: "Every time they make a deal they either break it or change it."
Rubio said he hoped the Iran-backed Houthis will de-escalate. "They got snookered by Iran."
The secretary of state also commented on the nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia, saying: "Even when the official announcement is made, it will have to go through a congressional notification process.
"And then we can talk more in depth about it, but suffice it to say that the US is always looking for co-operation agreements with allies."
He called Saudi Arabia "a strategic ally" of Washington.
Iranian state media report two dead in latest US strikes
Iranian state media reported that two people were killed and 11 wounded in US strikes in southern Iran, near the country's border with Iraq.
The deaths were reported as part of the latest wave of American attacks, which the US military said targeted "maritime capabilities, missile and drone storage facilities, coastal surveillance sites, and air defence assets".
Centcom said in a statement on X: "The strikes further degrade Iran's ability to attack civilian mariners and commercial vessels."
According to Al Jazeera, explosions were reported in cities including Bushehr, Ahvaz, Sirik and Shalamcheh.
Centcom earlier today posted on X, saying it completed the 12th night of strikes on Iran.
Iran's Revolutionary Guard defends its strikes on Gulf states
Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) defended its attacks on US military bases in Kuwait and Jordan, arguing that the strikes were aimed at American forces rather than the Gulf states themselves.
Responding to accusations that Iran had violated the sovereignty of countries where US bases are located, the IRGC said such claims were "completely incorrect", according to Mehr state news agency.
In a message to Kuwait, it said: "It is America that has violated your territorial integrity and sovereignty, not us."
The IRGC argued that US military bases in Gulf countries were effectively under American control and claimed local officials could not access them without US permission.
It said: "We are attacking lands occupied by the US army."
The group added that the bases were being used to carry out "crimes" against Iran and said: "It is our legal, religious and logical right to target the invader of our country."
In a separate statement directed at Jordan, the IRGC said: "In our opinion, the independence, territorial integrity and sovereignty of your government are completely respected."
new_text:
Donald Trump is in "revenge mode" with Iran and believes the only way to get through to its leadership is with military force, allies have said as the US military carried out a 13th consecutive day of strikes.
A senior administration official told the Wall Street Journal that the US president was losing patience and sees few good options in front of him besides continuing strikes.
Officials and those close to the president said he is increasingly frustrated that a conflict he expected to be wrapped up within weeks is still dragging on with no end in sight.
The US military is now reportedly moving weapons, medics and special forces to the Middle East to broaden options as hopes of a diplomatic resolution wane.
In a bid to break Iran's hold on the Strait of Hormuz, Trump also announced overnight that any damage sustained by ships as a result of Iranian strikes would be compensated with frozen Iranian assets held in the US.
Iran claims drone strikes on Kuwait
The Iranian army says it has launched another wave of drone attacks against US military facilities in Kuwait.
Sirens are also sounding in Bahrain, according to the country's interior ministry.
Watch: US military says it completed 13th night of strikes against Iran
The latest: Trump in 'revenge mode' as war drags on - report
Good morning. If you are just joining us, here's the latest:
- Donald Trump is increasingly frustrated that a war he thought would be over within weeks is dragging on with no end in sight, the WSJ reported, citing administration officials and people close to the US president.
- The outlet also reported that Trump is in "revenge mode" and believes Iran only understands force. It said the US was surging forces, medics and weaponry to the region to give more options if Washington chooses to escalate again.
- The US military said it had completed a 13th consecutive night of strikes on Iran in an effort to dislodge its hold on the Strait of Hormuz, while the Islamic Republic announced strikes on Jordan and sirens sounded in Bahrain.
- Trump announced that any damage done to ships by Iran would be compensated with frozen Iranian assets held by the US - a point of leverage earlier referenced in talks to end the war.
- The number of tankers crossing the Strait of Hormuz fell to just one on Thursday, the lowest level since 7 May. The very large crude carrier New Giant, loaded with 2 million barrels of Iraqi Basrah crude, exited the strait on Thursday and is due to arrive at China's Rizhao port by mid-August.
Trump says Iran war 'going extremely well' but warns regime has 'evil intentions'
Qatar Airways suspends flights to Bahrain, Kuwait and Erbil until 31 July
Qatar Airways has suspended passenger flights to Bahrain, Kuwait and Erbil until 31 July, the airline said in a statement on its website, as regional security tensions continue to affect air travel across the Gulf.
MAGA support for Trump's Iran war plummets, poll finds: 'Maximum level of frustration'
MAGA support for President Donald Trump's war against Iran has cratered over the past two months - with just 37 percent of his crumbling base now saying the conflict should continue even if it increases inflation, according to a new poll.
That's down from 50 percent since May, Politico said in a Wednesday report that came a day after Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth sparred with Democrats during a fiery Senate hearing over the cost of the war.
A person close to the White House told Politico that it's "at a maximum level of frustration right now," with Trump "fully aware that the only way to the victory that he wants is completely politically impossible."
Iran claims attacks on US military bases in Jordan and Bahrain
Iran's army has claimed attacks on US military facilities at Jordan's Al-Azraq base and Bahrain's Sheikh Isa air base, Iranian state media reported.
UN chief warns Middle East is 'getting out of control' and 'teetering on the edge of the unimaginable'
UN secretary general António Guterres has warned the Security Council that the Middle East conflict is "getting out of control".
"It is teetering on the edge of the unimaginable. The region is being pulled into an ever-widening circle of confrontation. One crisis feeds another. One escalation triggers the next," Mr Guterres told the Security Council.
Trump says he is close to decision on 'major military punishment' for Iran after Houthi tanker attacks
US president Donald Trump has said he is considering re-launching major combat operations against Iran and is close to a decision, telling Axios that Tehran "hasn't received enough pain yet."
Mr Trump's warning came after Yemen's Houthi fighters struck two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea, extending the Middle East war to a second major shipping chokepoint.
Mr Trump said he would hold Iran responsible for any further Houthi attacks, writing on social media: "If they do this again, the US will hold Iran responsible, in that the Houthis are a Surrogate and/or Proxy of Iran, and major military punishment will be inflicted upon Iran and, of course, the Houthis, themselves."
Sources in Iran and Yemen told Reuters that Iran had flown Revolutionary Guard commanders, military advisers and missile and drone-related equipment into Yemen days before the Houthis announced their Red Sea blockade. The Houthis dismissed the report.
In focus: Massive escalation or back to talks? The four options Trump has left on Iran
The US is failing to wear down Iran with strikes around the Strait of Hormuz and making no progress to resolve the conflict through diplomacy. Here are Donald Trump's apparent options:

Sirens are also sounding in Bahrain, according to the country's interior ministry.
